results.possibleIdentity: Richard Serra (frequently misspelled in contemporary documents as Richard Sierra) is an American sculptor and video artist who was an active member of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization in the late 1960s and early 1970s. On his E.A.T. membership form, he expressed interest in using rubber, neon, and electronics in his artistic practice. He also experimented with film through E.A.T., notably producing the 36-minute film 'Color Aid' (1970–1971). Serra was one of the artists selected by E.A.T. to contribute to 'The New York Collection for Stockholm Portfolio' (1973), a project that donated significant works of 1960s American art to the Moderna Museet in Sweden.

Rubén Sierra (1946–1998) was a Chicano playwright, actor, and director active in the late 1960s and 1970s. He gained national recognition for his 1968 play 'La Raza Pura, or Racial, Racial,' which was one of the first Latino plays broadcast on National Educational Television (the precursor to PBS). Sierra served as the founding artistic director of the multicultural Seattle Group Theatre and was a faculty member at the University of Washington Drama School. He was a central figure in the Chicano and multicultural theater movements of the late 20th century.

results.possibleIdentity: Robert O'Brien (born 1939) is a British-born artist and theatre designer who was active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. He trained in theatre design and worked as a full-time artist in London and Munich between 1964 and 1975 before moving to Hong Kong in 1976. In Hong Kong, he became a prominent figure in the local art ecology, teaching at institutions such as the University of Hong Kong's Department of Extra Mural Studies and the Academy of Performing Arts. His work often involved interdisciplinary approaches, such as mixing music with late Renaissance painting, and he was associated with experimental art communities and artist-led organizations. His early career in theatre design and his presence in the experimental art scenes of London and Munich in the late 1960s align with the timeframe and collaborative interests associated with organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.).

Robert O'Brien (1908–1997) was a prominent American film executive who served as the president and CEO of Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer (MGM) during the 1960s. While not an artist in the traditional sense, he played a critical role in the intersection of art and high technology by greenlighting and securing the massive $5 million budget for Stanley Kubrick's '2001: A Space Odyssey' (1968). His leadership at MGM during the late 1960s was defined by his recognition of the public's fascination with the Space Race and his willingness to invest in technologically ambitious film projects that pushed the boundaries of visual art and engineering.

Robert C. O'Brien (1918–1973), born Robert Leslie Conly, was an American author and journalist active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. He is best known for his award-winning children's literature, including 'Mrs. Frisby and the Rats of NIMH' (1971) and the post-apocalyptic 'Z for Zachariah'. In 1964, he authored 'Machines' as part of the LIFE Science Library, a book that explored the relationship between technology, human invention, and the arts. His work frequently engaged with themes of technological impact and scientific experimentation, mirroring the intellectual concerns of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement during that period.

results.possibleIdentity: Jay Bouton is an American interior designer and architect who was active in the late 1960s and 1970s. Originally from Denver, he studied at the Pratt Institute in Brooklyn, New York, where he was a senior student in 1971. Bouton's early career in the 1970s was centered in New York, where he contributed to professional design discourse, including correspondence with "Interiors" magazine regarding architectural and social issues. He later founded or led the firm Design Phase Ltd. (also known as designphase limited) and expanded his practice to the Pacific Rim, specializing in hotel and office design in regions such as Hong Kong, Thailand, and Burma. His work in the 1980s included high-profile joint ventures, such as collaborating with architect Remo Riva of Palmer & Turner on projects for the Hong Kong Land Co. Given his background at Pratt and his presence in the New York design scene during the late 1960s and early 1970s, he occupied the same professional and geographic milieu as the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, which frequently collaborated with designers and engineers from institutions like Pratt.

results.possibleIdentity: Franco Ciarlo (born March 28, 1939, in Rome) is an Italian-American painter and mixed-media artist known for his distinctive "fresco transfer technique." He studied architecture at the University of Rome and art at the Liceo Artistico before moving to the United States in 1964. After traveling through Mexico and Hawaii, he settled in New York City in 1967, where he became active in the contemporary art scene. His work often utilizes materials such as gauze, glue, and paper to create textured, large-scale canvases that mimic the appearance of ancient frescoes. Ciarlo's activity in the late 1960s and early 1970s is well-documented; his work "Structure Black, Orange, Purple" is referenced in the Dorothy C. Miller papers at the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) archives. These archives also contain materials related to the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, supporting a connection to the interdisciplinary and experimental art circles of that era. Throughout his career, Ciarlo has exhibited at prominent venues such as the Iolas-Jackson Gallery in New York and has held teaching positions at the Pratt Institute and Parsons School of Design. His work is represented in collections including the Everson Museum of Art and private collections such as that of David Rockefeller, Jr.
